answer.rationale: Pan-canonical Greek lexical network across multiple authors directly predicates sinlessness of Christ. 2 Cor 5:21 (τὸν μὴ γνόντα ἁμαρτίαν, 'the one who knew no sin', gnonta G1097 + hamartian G266), Heb 4:15 (χωρὶς ἁμαρτίας, 'without sin', choris G5565 + hamartias G266), Heb 7:26 (ὅσιος ἄκακος ἀμίαντος G3741 G172 G283), 1 Pet 1:19 (ἀμνοῦ ἀμώμου καὶ ἀσπίλου G286 G299 G784), 1 Pet 2:22 (ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ ἐποίησεν G266 G4160), and 1 John 3:5 (ἁμαρτία ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν). Isa 53:9 (חָמָס H2555, מִרְמָה H4820) supplies the Hebrew prophetic substrate. Christ's own Johannine challenge in John 8:46 ('which of you convicts me of sin?', elenchei G1651) confirms the self-attestation.
answer.notes: Christ's sinlessness is the load-bearing precondition for substitutionary atonement. The logic of 2 Cor 5:21 is conditional: only because Christ knew no sin (γνόντα ἁμαρτίαν, aorist participle of completed non-experience) could God make him 'sin for us'. 1 Pet 1:19 grounds redemption in the lamb-imagery of Lev sacrificial code: only an unblemished (ἀμώμου G299) lamb could ransom, and Peter applies this precisely to Christ's blood. Deny Christ's sinlessness and the cross becomes either an unjust execution or a martyrdom, not an atonement. Public-record carriers of denial: any movement positing Christ committed actual sin places itself outside historic Christianity (this is essentially unattested in any continuing major lineage). The Edward Irving 1830s 'fallen humanity of Christ' controversy posited that Christ assumed a fallen sin-prone nature while never actually sinning; most adherents still affirm actual sinlessness, and the controversy concerns nature not act. The bar in this question is the act and the nature with respect to actual sin (committed no sin, knew no sin, in him is no sin), which is uniformly canonical. Some hyper-liberal academic reductionism that recasts Jesus as a fallible moral teacher implicitly denies sinlessness but rarely confesses it openly. Fruit-vs-precision nuance: most rank-and-file believers across all historic lineages confess Christ as sinless without being able to recite the lexical evidence; the proposition is one of the broadest catholic agreements in church history. The lexical-pan-canonical bar is fully met; cult-marker status holds at the canonical level.

stem_audit.notes: Statement 'Jesus Christ was without sin in His thoughts, words, and deeds throughout His earthly life' asserts the verdict, but the three-fold partition (thoughts, words, deeds) is a fair restatement of the canonical pattern: 'in him is no sin' (nature / thoughts, 1 John 3:5), 'no deceit was found in his mouth' (words, 1 Pet 2:22 quoting Isa 53:9), and 'committed no sin' (deeds, 1 Pet 2:22). The proposition tracks the lexical evidence rather than smuggling confessional vocabulary.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: Jesus Christ never sinned. He never sinned in what he thought, said, or did, his whole life. The strongest reason from Scripture is that the apostles say this plainly and repeatedly in different books and different ways. Paul says in 2 Corinthians 5:21 that God made Jesus 'to be sin for us, who knew no sin'. Peter says in 1 Peter 2:22 that Jesus 'committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth'. John says in 1 John 3:5 that 'in him is no sin'. Hebrews 4:15 says Jesus was tempted in every way like we are, 'yet without sin'. Jesus himself, in John 8:46, challenges his enemies face to face and asks 'which of you convicts me of sin?', and nobody can. The strongest counterargument is Hebrews 4:15 itself, because if Jesus was really tempted, some readers ask whether the temptation involved some inner pull toward sin. The verdict handles this by noting that the verse itself answers the question in the same sentence: tempted in every way, yet without sin. Temptation from outside is not the same as sinful desire inside. Some also point to Isaiah 53:9, which says Jesus 'had done no violence, neither was any deceit in his mouth', a positive Old Testament prediction that the suffering servant would be morally innocent. This doctrine matters because the whole logic of the cross depends on it. Only a sinless person could die for the sins of others. If Jesus had any sin of his own, he could not pay for anyone else's. This is why the New Testament calls him a 'lamb without blemish and without spot' in 1 Peter 1:19, picking up the Old Testament rule that sacrificial lambs had to be perfect.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every major historic Christian lineage confesses that Jesus Christ was without sin. This is one of the least contested doctrines in church history. The patristic fathers (Athanasius, Augustine, Chrysostom, John of Damascus) all teach Christ's sinlessness as essential to the incarnation and atonement. The Catholic Church teaches in its Catechism that Christ is the spotless Lamb of God and that his sinlessness is what enables him to be the once-for-all sacrifice. Lutherans confess in the Augsburg Confession Article III that Christ, born of the Virgin Mary, made satisfaction for our sins by his death, a function that presupposes his own sinlessness. Anglicans state it most explicitly in Article 15 of the 39 Articles, which is titled 'Of Christ alone without Sin' and says Christ 'in the truth of our nature was made like unto us in all things, sin only except, from which he was clearly void, both in his flesh, and in his spirit'. The Reformed confessions (Westminster, Belgic, Heidelberg) state Christ was 'yet without sin'. Methodists in the Articles of Religion describe Christ as the sacrifice for sins, presupposing sinlessness. Anabaptists at Schleitheim and Dordrecht confess the same Christ of the apostolic witness. Pentecostals in the Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths confess the sinless life of Christ explicitly. Eastern Orthodoxy teaches that Christ assumed our human nature without sin and remained sinless. Public-record carriers of denial are rare and largely outside any continuing major Christian tradition. Any teaching that posits Christ committed actual sin places itself outside historic Christianity. Some hyper-liberal academic positions reduce Jesus to a fallible human teacher, which functionally denies sinlessness, but they rarely confess so directly. The Edward Irving 1830s controversy about whether Christ assumed a 'fallen' human nature is a side question about the nature he assumed, not about whether he actually sinned; even Irving and his followers affirmed Christ never sinned in act. On the bare proposition that Jesus Christ was without sin, historic Christianity speaks with one voice.

scripture.key_term: τὸν μὴ γνόντα ἁμαρτίαν; gnonta G1097 + hamartian G266
scripture.force: gnonta = aorist active participle of ginosko, 'having not known sin' in the experiential sense. The construction predicates total non-experience of sin of Christ. Paul's substitutionary logic depends on this: God made the sinless one to be sin so that we might become God's righteousness in him

scripture.key_term: χωρὶς ἁμαρτίας; choris G5565 + hamartias G266
scripture.force: choris = separation, apart from, without. The phrase 'without sin' qualifies the entire scope of Christ's temptation: tempted in every respect like us, yet on the far side of sin. The preposition signals complete exemption despite comprehensive temptation

scripture.key_term: ὅσιος ἄκακος ἀμίαντος; hosios G3741 + akakos G172 + amiantos G283
scripture.force: Three-fold lexical predication: hosios = holy, pious; akakos = innocent, guileless, without evil; amiantos = undefiled, unstained. Followed by kechorismenos apo ton hamartolon (G5563 + G268), 'separated from sinners'. The high-priestly qualifications. Lexical density at maximum

scripture.key_term: ἀμνοῦ ἀμώμου καὶ ἀσπίλου; amnos G286 + amomos G299 + aspilos G784
scripture.force: amnos = lamb (sacrificial term, picking up Levitical code); amomos = without blemish (the LXX term for Levitical unblemished offering, Lev 22:21); aspilos = without spot, unstained. Peter applies Old Testament sacrificial purity terminology directly to Christ's moral and physical sinlessness as the basis of redemption

scripture.key_term: ἁμαρτίαν οὐκ ἐποίησεν οὐδὲ εὑρέθη δόλος; hamartia G266 + epoiesen G4160 + dolos G1388
scripture.force: Quoting Isa 53:9. 'Who committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth'. Aorist epoiesen reports completed non-action: across his entire earthly life, no sin was done. Pairs the deed (no sin committed) with the word (no deceit in his mouth)

scripture.key_term: ἁμαρτία ἐν αὐτῷ οὐκ ἔστιν; hamartia G266 + en G1722 + auto G846
scripture.force: Negated existential present: 'sin is not in him'. Predicates ongoing non-presence of sin in Christ's person, not merely non-action. Speaks to nature as well as conduct. The strongest single statement on Christ's interior sinlessness

scripture.key_term: τίς ἐξ ὑμῶν ἐλέγχει με περὶ ἁμαρτίας; elenchei G1651 + hamartias G266
scripture.force: elenchei = to convict, expose, prove guilty. Present indicative active. Jesus's open challenge to his hostile audience: prove me guilty of any sin. The absence of a response in the narrative is itself canonical evidence. Christ's own self-attestation of sinlessness

scripture.key_term: לֹא־חָמָס עָשָׂה וְלֹא מִרְמָה בְּפִיו; chamas H2555 + mirmah H4820
scripture.force: chamas = violence, wrong, injustice; mirmah = deceit, treachery. Both negated: 'although he had done no violence, neither was any deceit in his mouth'. The Old Testament prophetic prediction of the suffering servant's moral innocence. Foundation for 1 Pet 2:22 which quotes it directly

scripture.key_term: ἐν ἐμοὶ οὐκ ἔχει οὐδέν; ouk echei ouden G3756 G2192 G3762
scripture.force: Jesus speaking of the ruler of this world: 'he has nothing in me'. The negation predicates that Satan finds no foothold, no point of moral accusation, no claim of ownership over Christ. Concordance reinforcement of the interior sinlessness theme

scripture.key_term: ἁμαρτίαν ἐποίησεν; hamartia G266 + epoiesen G4160
scripture.force: The second half of 2 Cor 5:21: God 'made him to be sin' (or 'sin offering'). This is the substitution-clause that depends on the first half. Without the sinless one, there is no substitute. Counter-reading would be: God made the sinful one to be sin, which is nonsensical and self-defeating for atonement

Christ offered himself 'without blemish to God'. amomos again, the same Levitical-sacrificial term used in 1 Pet 1:19. Concordance reinforcement: the New Testament uses Levitical purity vocabulary for Christ's sinlessness in multiple independent epistles

scripture.key_term: τί με λέγεις ἀγαθόν; οὐδεὶς ἀγαθὸς εἰ μὴ εἷς ὁ θεός
scripture.force: Jesus to the rich young ruler: 'Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone'. Sometimes cited as Jesus disclaiming sinlessness or goodness. The grammatico-historical reading is the opposite: Jesus is challenging the man to recognize the implication of calling him good, namely that he is God. The verse complicates surface reading but on examination supports the canonical doctrine

scripture.key_term: πειρασθῆναι ὑπὸ τοῦ διαβόλου; peirasthenai G3985
scripture.force: Jesus was led up by the Spirit into the wilderness 'to be tempted by the devil'. peirazo = test, tempt. Real temptation is predicated of Christ, which Heb 4:15 confirms. Complicates a simple reading because temptation is real, but the canonical pattern is unanimous: real temptation without sin

Jesus 'grew in wisdom and stature and in favor with God and man'. Sometimes raised as a complication: if Jesus grew, did he start imperfect? The grammatico-historical reading is developmental, not moral: he grew in human maturity without ever sinning. Concordance check: proekopto means progress or advance, not correction from error

hermeneutics.notes: Verdict rests on a pan-canonical Greek lemma network: hamartia G266 negated of Christ across multiple authors (Paul, the writer of Hebrews, Peter, John), Levitical sacrificial purity vocabulary (amomos G299, aspilos G784, amnos G286) reapplied typologically to Christ in 1 Pet 1:19 and Heb 9:14, high-priestly qualification terms (hosios G3741, akakos G172, amiantos G283) in Heb 7:26, and a Hebrew prophetic substrate (chamas H2555, mirmah H4820 negated in Isa 53:9, quoted directly in 1 Pet 2:22). The proposition is settled at the lexical level and requires no exotic framework. Covenant, dispensational, new-covenant, and progressive-covenantalism hermeneutics all confess Christ's sinlessness identically. Complicating texts (Mark 10:18, Matt 4:1, Luke 2:52) yield to grammatico-historical reading without strain. Analogia scripturae is invoked: 1 Pet 2:22 quotes Isa 53:9, binding the testaments at one verse, and the Levitical sacrificial typology is reapplied across Hebrews and 1 Peter with matching vocabulary.

counter_witness.anchor: Athanasius, On the Incarnation, and the Cappadocian fathers on Christ's sinless humanity
counter_witness.key_phrase: Patristic consensus that Christ took our nature in its fullness while remaining without sin, otherwise the atonement would be impossible. Foundational to the Chalcedonian Definition (451)

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church on Christ as the spotless Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Catechism teaches Christ as the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, the once-for-all sacrifice whose sinlessness qualifies him to atone for the sins of others

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession Article III, Of the Son of God
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ by His death has made satisfaction for our sins. The satisfaction logic of Article III presupposes Christ's own sinlessness; a sinful sacrificer could not satisfy

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles of Religion, Article 15, Of Christ alone without Sin
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ in the truth of our nature was made like unto us in all things, sin only except, from which he was clearly void, both in his flesh, and in his spirit

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession 8.2, Of Christ the Mediator
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ the Son of God took upon himself man's nature, with all the essential properties and common infirmities thereof, yet without sin. Standard Reformed confession of Christ's sinlessness in the incarnation

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion, Article II, Of the Word, or Son of God, Who Was Made Very Man
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ truly suffered, was crucified, dead, and buried, to be a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for actual sins of men. Presupposes Christ's own sinlessness as the basis for sacrificial efficacy

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession Article 4, Of the Coming of Christ into this World
counter_witness.key_phrase: Anabaptist confession of Christ as the perfect, holy, sinless Son of God sent into the world for the salvation of mankind

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths, Article 2, on the Deity of the Lord Jesus Christ
counter_witness.key_phrase: Pentecostal confession of the sinless life of Christ as one of the essentials of the deity and saving work of Jesus Christ, alongside virgin birth and substitutionary atonement

counter_witness.anchor: OCA, The Orthodox Faith, on the Person of Jesus Christ
counter_witness.key_phrase: Orthodox teaching that Christ assumed our human nature without sin, was tempted in every way as we are yet without sin, and remained sinless throughout his earthly life

web.quote: 2 Cor 5:21 ton me gnonta hamartian, aorist active participle gnonta G1097 of ginosko + hamartian G266; predicates total non-experience of sin of Christ
Heb 4:15 choris hamartias G5565 + G266; preposition signals complete separation or exemption from sin despite comprehensive temptation
Heb 7:26 hosios G3741 akakos G172 amiantos G283 kechorismenos G5563 apo ton hamartolon G268; five-fold lexical predication of high-priestly purity
1 Pet 1:19 amnou amomou kai aspilou G286 G299 G784; Levitical sacrificial purity vocabulary applied to Christ as the unblemished lamb of redemption
1 Pet 2:22 hamartian ouk epoiesen G266 G4160 + oude heurethe dolos G1388; quoting Isa 53:9, predicates non-action of sin and non-presence of deceit
1 John 3:5 hamartia en auto ouk estin G266 G1722 G846; present indicative active negated existential, 'sin is not in him'; speaks to nature not merely conduct
John 8:46 tis ex hymon elenchei me peri hamartias G1651 G266; Jesus's open challenge, present active, prove me guilty of any sin
Isa 53:9 chamas H2555 + mirmah H4820 both negated; Hebrew prophetic substrate of the suffering servant's moral innocence, quoted directly in 1 Pet 2:22
hamartia G266, 173 NT occurrences, negated of Christ in John 8:46, Rom 8:3, 2 Cor 5:21, Heb 4:15, Heb 7:26, Heb 9:28, 1 Pet 2:22, 1 John 3:5; pan-canonical reach
Anglican 39 Articles Article 15: Christ in the truth of our nature was made like unto us in all things, sin only except, from which he was clearly void, both in his flesh, and in his spirit
Augsburg Confession Article III on the Son of God: who by his death has made satisfaction for our sins. Satisfaction logic presupposes Christ's own sinlessness
UMC Articles of Religion Article II describes Christ as the sacrifice not only for original guilt but also for actual sins of men, presupposing his own sinlessness
